issues.description: lower_string_literal regressed from `.unwrap_or(text)` to `.unwrap()`. The original was safe by design — the inline comment 'Strip outer quotes safely (error recovery can produce malformed nodes)' documents that tree-sitter error recovery can hand this function nodes whose text does not begin or end with `"`. With `.unwrap()`, any such malformed node will panic the parser. This is a behavioral regression that will crash the parser on inputs the original happily handled (e.g. unclosed strings).
issues.suggested_fix: Restore `.unwrap_or(text)` (or equivalently use `Option::map`/`unwrap_or_else` while preserving the fallback). The function returns `Option<Expr>`, so if you really want to reject malformed nodes, return `None` via `?` instead of panicking — but the original behavior of treating the raw text as the string contents on stripping failure is the intended robust path.

issues.description: The existing edge case test `unclosed_string_does_not_panic` (and the comment block at line 28: '── C2: lower_string_literal should not panic on malformed input ────') explicitly verifies that this code path does NOT panic on malformed input. The new `.unwrap()` will cause this regression test to fail. This test was specifically added to lock in the safe behavior that this diff is removing.
issues.suggested_fix: Run `cargo test -p reify-syntax edge_case_tests` before merging. Restore the safe quote-stripping logic so the regression test continues to pass.

issues.description: The new lower_single_quoted_string helper has the same panic flaw — `.strip_prefix('\'').and_then(|s| s.strip_suffix('\'')).unwrap()` will panic on any malformed single-quoted token. Tree-sitter error recovery routinely yields nodes that are missing one of their delimiters; this code will turn that into a hard crash.
issues.suggested_fix: Mirror the safe fallback pattern (or return `None` via `?`). Better yet, factor out a shared helper: `fn strip_quotes(text: &str, q: char) -> &str { text.strip_prefix(q).and_then(|s| s.strip_suffix(q)).unwrap_or(text) }` and call it from both lowering functions.

issues.description: lower_single_quoted_string is never called anywhere in the crate. `lower_expr` (line 1697) only dispatches `"string_literal"` to lower_string_literal — there is no match arm for a single-quoted node kind, and ripgrep finds zero call sites for `lower_single_quoted_string`. The function is dead code as wired. Either the dispatch in `lower_expr` is missing (the change is incomplete) or this function should not exist.
issues.suggested_fix: If single-quoted strings are intended to be supported, add the appropriate node-kind arm to `lower_expr` (e.g. `"single_quoted_string" => self.lower_single_quoted_string(node)`), confirm the tree-sitter grammar emits that kind, and add tests. If not, delete the function — don't ship dead code that will rot.

issues.description: No tests are added for lower_single_quoted_string. The crate has a dedicated edge_case_tests.rs file specifically for string-literal robustness; the new code path has zero coverage there. Even ignoring that the function is currently unwired, shipping a parser-lowering routine without a normal-case test, an empty-string test, and a malformed-input test is below the bar already established for sibling code (see edge_case_tests.rs C2 block).
issues.suggested_fix: Add at least three tests mirroring the existing string_literal_normal / empty_string_literal / unclosed_string_does_not_panic trio for single-quoted literals — once the function is actually wired into lower_expr.

issues.description: lower_string_literal and lower_single_quoted_string differ only in the quote character. Two near-identical 12-line functions invite drift (the panic bug here will need to be fixed in two places, and any future fix to one will need to be remembered for the other).
issues.suggested_fix: Extract a private helper, e.g. `fn lower_quoted_string(&self, node: tree_sitter::Node, quote: char) -> Option<Expr>`, and have both call sites pass `'"'` or `'\''`. Or factor out just the stripping: `fn strip_outer(text: &str, q: char) -> &str { text.strip_prefix(q).and_then(|s| s.strip_suffix(q)).unwrap_or(text) }`.

issues.description: The function signature returns `Option<Expr>` but never returns `None`. If a malformed node really should be rejected (rather than passed through as-is), use `?` so callers can observe the failure and emit a parse error. Using unwrap_or(text) silently masks malformed input as a successful string literal — which is the documented intent here, but worth flagging given the project's `feedback_silent_defaults_pattern.md` norm.
issues.suggested_fix: Either keep the unwrap_or fallback (current intent — robustness over strictness, fine for error-recovery paths) or convert both stripping steps to `?` and document the shift. Don't introduce a third behavior (`.unwrap()` panic), which is what this diff does.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The diff introduces a blocking panic regression in lower_string_literal by replacing the deliberate `.unwrap_or(text)` safety net with `.unwrap()`, directly contradicting both the inline comment ("Strip outer quotes safely — error recovery can produce malformed nodes") and the existing edge_case_tests.rs `unclosed_string_does_not_panic` regression test (which will fail on this change). The new lower_single_quoted_string helper repeats the same panic flaw and is additionally dead code — `lower_expr` has no dispatch arm for single-quoted strings and no other call site exists, so the function as written cannot be reached. There is also no test coverage for the new helper, and the two near-identical functions invite drift. The robust fix is to restore the unwrap_or fallback (or use `?`), factor the shared stripping logic into one helper, wire single-quoted strings into lower_expr if that's the intent, and add the corresponding edge_case_tests.rs coverage.
